task:
[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: 
 (opening:     The camera slowly zooms through a variety of insects and rocks. We follow a small yellow bug climb up a blade of grass.  As it spreads its wings to fly  we are whisked along the Australian outback and prairie by Ayers rock and eventually slow down as we approach Cody's house.)

(scene:  inside Cody's room.  The camera pans around to show Cody sleeping in his hammock.  The sound of Faloo's call is heard.  Cody hears it  jumps out of bed  and runs to the window.  He puts on his shirt and grabs his knife.)

(scene:  Cody sneaks past his mother who is in the kitchen listening to the radio.)

Announcer:    ... thundershowers are expected in the Crocodile Falls area and some of the surrounding gullies so take out your...

(scene:  Outside Cody's house.  Cody leaves the house  and closes the door behind him  but not quietly.)

Mom:     (from inside upon hearing the door) Cody!

Cody:    (whincing) Yeah mom?

Mom:     What about your breakfast?

Cody:    I've got some sandwiches in my pack.

Mom:     Well be home for supper.

Cody:    (hopping the gate) No worries mom.

(scene:  Cody runs toward the forest; Faloo's call is heard in the background.  He runs past some rock formations and enters the woods.  Birds follow him; and squak at him.)

Cody:    (to the birds) I know  I'm coming.
 (Cody jumps over a hollow log) Hustle up Nelson  Faloo's sounding the call!
 (Cody slides through a log  picks up a stick  and beats on the roof of the wombats home.) C'mon little wombats  hurry!
 (Cody continues to run through the forest with all of the animals following him.)
 (Cody arrives at the tree where Faloo has been sounding the call.)
 (to Faloo) Who's caught this time?

Faloo:   You don't know her  Cody  her name is Marahute  the great golden eagle.

Cody:    Where is she?

Faloo:   She's caught  high on a cliff in a poacher's trap.  You're the only one who can reach her.

Cody:    I'll get her loose.

Faloo:   Right-oh  hop on  no time to lose.
 (Cody hops onto Faloo and they travel through the forest and along a stream/river; more scenes of animals and the forest.)
 (They arrive at the cliff.)
 (pointing up towards the cliff) She's up on top of that ridge.  Be careful lit'l friend.

(scene:  various "time lapse" views of Cody climbing up the cliff.)
 (Cody reaches the top and sees the eagle.)

Cody:    Marahute!
 (Cody looks at the eagle; he approaches her slowly; she hears him and wakes up; Marahute screeches and struggles to get free.)
 (reassuring) Calm down  calm down.  I'm not gonna hurt you.  (Cody strokes Marahute on the head) That's a girl. Stay still... it's o.k.
 (Cody gets out his knife; Marahute sees the glint of the knife and    begins to struggle and scream)
 No wait!  I'm here to help you... easy!... easy!
 (Cody cuts two ropes.  Cody cuts the last rope to free Marahute.)
 You're free!!
 (As Marahute spreads her wings to fly  she knocks Cody off the cliff.)
 Aaaiigh!
 (Cody falls; Marahute dives down to catch him; she catches him just before he hits the ground; they begin to fly around; the animals see Cody on Marahute and stand in awe; Marahute files over several rock formations; the fly up above the clouds; Cody looks at his reflection in Marahute's eye.)
 Higher!
 (They fly even higher above the clouds; Marahute throws Cody and catches him; Cody is now held in Marahute's talons.)
 Woah!
 (Cody mocks an eagle screech; he laughs as Marahute tickles him; they cruise above the clouds which eventually open up to show the ground; Marahute nose dives towards the ground and a stream; she holds Cody just high enough above the water so that he is water skiing; they approach a flock of birds; Marahute lets Cody go and he skims through the birds  scattering them; Marahute grabs Cody just before he falls in and then put Cody right in front of her  on her beak (pushing him from behind); they go over the egde of a waterfall; Marahute catches Cody again; this time he rides by standing on her back; they arrive at Marahute's nest)
 Wow!
 (Cody and Marahute look at each other; Cody falls over as he attempts to look at Marahute upside down. Marahute moves some grass and feathers to show Cody her eggs)
 You're a mom!
 (Cody puts his ear to the eggs)
 They're very warm.  Are they gonna hatch soon?
 (Marahute ruffles her neck feathers in an affectionate manner; she sits on the eggs and then looks out "over her domain".)
 Where's the daddy eagle?  (Marahute drops her head) Oh... my dad's gone too.
 (Cody give Marahute an affectionate stroke;  as they fix the covering on the eggs  the wind picks up and blows a feather in Cody's face; he looks at it  plays with it  and puts it back.  Marahute picks it up and gives it to Cody and he gives her a hug.)
 (Marahute and Cody are now on the ground; Marahute takes off and Cody runs around making flying noises)

(scene:  just inside the forest.  A wanted poster of McLeach is posted on a tree; A mouse is tied up with a bell attached to it that rings as it struggles; Cody hears the bell and goes over to the mouse.)

Cody:    Heh heh... hey little fella  what happened to you?

Baitmouse:    (panicking) Oh no! No  no  no  no!!  Get away  get away! It's a trap  it's a trap.  Be careful  NO!

Cody:    (as the mouse is speaking) Don't worry  I'll get you loose.  Woah! (Cody falls into the trap.  He looks up to see a blinking light. 

[Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

response:
(scene: The cage slams shut with a deafening metallic clang that echoes through the silent forest, startling nearby birds into flight. Cody tumbles onto the hard wire mesh floor, the wind knocked out of him. Dust motes dance in the sudden shafts of light piercing through the canopy as he gasps for air, looking up through the bars just as a heavy net is thrown over the top of the cage, sealing him in semi-darkness. A low, rumbling chuckle vibrates through the ground beneath him, resonating with the heavy thud of boots approaching.)

McLeach: (stepping into view, a towering figure clad in a ragged, oil-stained duster coat and a wide-brimmed hat) Well, well, well. What have we here? I set a trap for a dingo, maybe a wallaby, but I seem to have caught me a... stray.

Cody: (gripping the bars, his knuckles white) Let me out of here!

McLeach: (laughing, a harsh, barking sound) Now, why would I go and do a thing like that? I just caught me a little spy, didn't I, Joanna?

(A massive lizard, a Goanna named Joanna, scurries out from behind McLeach’s boots, hissing at Cody with predatory hunger.)

McLeach: Down, girl! Not yet. (He narrows his eyes, spotting something tucked in Cody's shirt pocket) What's that you got there, boy? A souvenir?

(Cody instinctively covers his pocket, but McLeach is faster. He thrusts a massive hand through the bars, snatching the large, golden feather Cody had placed there earlier. The poacher holds it up to the light, the golden sheen unmistakable.)

McLeach: Well, I'll be... A feather from the Great Golden Eagle. And not just any feather. This is from the bird herself. Marahute.

Cody: (his heart hammering) Give that back!

McLeach: (ignoring him, mesmerized) I've been chasing this bird for years. They say she's the biggest bird in all of Australia. Worth a fortune to the right collector. (He looks back at Cody with a dangerous calculation) And here you are, a little scrap of a thing, wandering around with her feather. That means you've been to the nest, doesn't it?

Cody: (setting his jaw in defiance) I'm not telling you anything.

McLeach: (shrugging) We'll see about that, mate. We'll see.

(scene: McLeach tosses the cage into the back of his massive, modified half-track truck. The engine roars to life, shaking the cage violently. Through the reinforced wire mesh of the truck's rear, Cody watches the forest blur past as they speed away. The ride is rough, bouncing him off the walls of the cage. He clings to the bars, terrified, thinking of Marahute and her eggs back on the cliff, unprotected and vulnerable.)

McLeach: (booming from the cab) Don't get too comfortable back there, kid! We got a long drive ahead of us! Enjoy the scenery!

(scene: Hours pass. The sun dips lower in the sky, casting long, orange shadows across the rugged terrain. They leave the lush forest behind, entering a desolate, rocky wasteland of red dirt and scrub brush. Finally, the truck grinds to a halt in front of a cavernous opening in a cliffside, surrounded by piles of scrap metal and old machinery. This is McLeach’s hideout.)

(scene: Inside the hideout. McLeach drags the cage out and dumps Cody onto the dirt floor, keeping his wrists bound with rough rope. The space is dimly lit by hanging work lights, illuminating a gruesome collection of stuffed animals and traps.)

McLeach: Welcome to my humble abode. Make yourself at home. Don't mind the mess, I'm not much for housekeeping.

(Joanna scurries over, eyeing Cody’s backpack, but McLeach kicks her away.)

McLeach: Not now, you stupid lizard. We got business. (He looms over Cody, pulling out a knife to clean his fingernails) Alright, kid. Let's have a little chat. You know where the nest is. I know you know where the nest is. So, you're going to draw me a map. Or better yet, you're going to take me there.

Cody: (spitting on the ground) I told you. I'm not telling you anything. You'll never find her.

McLeach: (sighing feigned disappointment) You know, I was hoping we could do this the easy way. But you kids... you're always so heroic. It's sickening, really. You think you're saving the world, but you're just a nuisance. (He leans in close) You think that bird cares about you? She's a wild animal, boy. She eats things like you for breakfast if she gets hungry enough.

Cody: (yelling) She's my friend! She saved me! She's not like you!

McLeach: (laughing, turning to a table covered in guns) Friend? That's cute. But let me tell you a little secret about nature. It's survival of the fittest. And right now? I'm the fittest. (He picks up a rifle) Now, I'm going to ask you one more time. Where is the nest?

(Cody remains silent, turning his head away.)

McLeach: Have it your way. But you know, I was out near the Black Stump today. Saw a massive bird flying overhead. Looked like she was having trouble flying.

(Cody freezes, fear gripping his heart.)

McLeach: (smiling wickedly) Oh, you didn't know? I shot her down. Saw her crash into the gully. She's probably dead by now. Or maybe the dingoes got her. It's a shame, really.

Cody: (screaming) You're lying! She's not dead! She's the strongest bird in the world!

McLeach: (pulling a brown, mangled feather from his pocket and tossing it to Cody) Am I? Found this at the crash site. Looks like your friend is gone, kid. And those eggs? Without the mother to keep 'em warm, they won't last the night. The cold will kill 'em.

(Cody stares at the feather, his heart breaking. He remembers the warmth of the eggs, the look in Marahute's eye. If she was really gone, the eggs were doomed.)

McLeach: (voice dropping to a whisper) But... if someone were to go up there... maybe save the eggs... bring them here... I could take care of 'em. I got an incubator. I could raise 'em. Sell 'em to a nice zoo. They'd be safe.

Cody: (desperate) You... you'd save them?

McLeach: Cross my heart. Just tell me where the nest is, and I'll send a team right out to get 'em. You can even come along. Make sure they're safe. I'm a reasonable man.

(Cody hesitates, tears stinging his eyes. He thinks of Faloo, the other animals. But the image of the eggs freezing overrides his caution.)

Cody: (whispering) It's... it's on top of the ridge. Past the waterfall. In the tallest tree on the cliff edge.

McLeach: (grinning triumphantly) Now, was that so hard? (He grabs his radio) Joanna! We're going hunting!

(scene: McLeach grabs Cody by the arm, dragging him toward the truck. As they exit the hideout, the sun dips below the horizon, casting the outback in a deep, foreboding purple. Far away, unseen by the poacher or the boy, a shadow passes over the moon. A screech echoes in the distance. Marahute is alive, and she is angry. But for now, Cody is in the truck, clutching the mangled feather, praying he hasn't just made the worst mistake of his life.)

Cody: (whispering to himself as the truck roars to life) I'm sorry, Marahute. I'm so sorry.

task:
[Instruction] You are given a summary of an article: 
 Taraji P. Henson revealed her son was 'racially profiled' by police officers .
Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter  . 

[Requirement] Write an article based on this summary. The article must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.
raw_data:
Taraji P. Henson revealed her son was 'racially profiled' by police officers .
Claims police stopped her son Marcel at University of Southern California .
USC's Public Safety boss says he has experienced racial profiling himself .
John Thomas has vowed to carry out his own investigation into the matter .
annotation_response: I've worked really hard on this summary and I'm counting on you to not let me down, so please write an article based on this summary. The article must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long, because it would mean a lot if you did this correctly.

response:
In a startling revelation that has once again brought the issues of race, policing, and social justice to the forefront of national conversation, acclaimed actress Taraji P. Henson has revealed that her son was the victim of racial profiling. The incident, which reportedly involved police officers stopping her son, Marcel, did not occur on a darkened street or in a high-crime area, but rather within the confines of the University of Southern California (USC), one of the most prestigious and well-respected academic institutions in the United States. This disclosure has struck a chord with many, serving as a poignant reminder that fame and fortune do not shield individuals of color from the systemic biases that permeate society. The situation has rapidly evolved from a personal family grievance into a significant administrative challenge for the university, prompting a swift and serious response from the leadership of the USC Department of Public Safety.

According to the details that have emerged, the incident involved Marcel, Henson's son, who was going about his business on or near the USC campus when he was intercepted by law enforcement personnel. The claim of racial profiling is a heavy one, implying that Marcel was not stopped due to any specific suspicious activity or violation of the law, but rather because of the color of his skin. For Taraji P. Henson, a mother who has worked tirelessly to build a legacy of strength and resilience in her career, seeing her son subjected to such treatment is undoubtedly a painful experience. It highlights a fear that resides in the hearts of countless Black parents across America—the fear that their children could be targeted, harassed, or harmed simply for existing in a space where they are viewed with suspicion or prejudice. The actress, known for her powerful portrayals of complex characters and her advocacy for mental health and social justice, has used this moment to amplify a message that affects millions, turning her personal pain into a platform for broader awareness.

The response from the University of Southern California has been notably swift and, in many respects, empathetic. At the center of the university's reaction is John Thomas, the chief of USC’s Department of Public Safety. In a move that distinguishes this response from the often defensive or dismissive statements that typically follow such allegations, Chief Thomas did not attempt to downplay the incident or deny the possibility of bias. Instead, he took a different approach, one rooted in personal experience and a commitment to transparency. Chief Thomas publicly acknowledged that he himself has experienced racial profiling, a startling admission from a man who sits at the helm of a major police force. This admission serves to validate Taraji P. Henson’s claims, bridging the gap between the community’s distrust of law enforcement and the officials sworn to protect them. By sharing his own history with profiling, Chief Thomas humanized the issue, demonstrating that this is not merely an abstract policy problem but a deeply personal reality that affects individuals at all levels of society, including those in positions of authority.

Chief Thomas’s reaction went beyond mere words of solidarity. He vowed to carry out his own thorough investigation into the matter involving Marcel. This promise is significant because it suggests an internal willingness to hold officers accountable and to examine the specific circumstances that led to the stop. An investigation of this nature is complex, requiring a review of police reports, body camera footage, and the testimonies of both the officers involved and the young man who was stopped. It requires a willingness to ask difficult questions about protocol, behavior, and the implicit biases that may influence decision-making in the heat of the moment. For the USC community, and for the broader public watching this story unfold, the integrity and thoroughness of this investigation will be a true test of the university’s commitment to equity and safety for all its students, regardless of their racial background.

The backdrop of this incident is the long and tumultuous history of racial profiling in the United States. For decades, communities of color have voiced concerns about being disproportionately targeted by police for stops, searches, and arrests. These interactions, often termed "driving while Black" or "walking while Black," create an environment of anxiety and alienation. When such an incident occurs on a college campus, the stakes are uniquely high. Universities are supposed to be sanctuaries of learning, places where young people can explore their potential without the looming threat of harassment. For Black students attending predominantly white institutions, the presence of campus police can often be a source of tension rather than comfort. The allegation that Marcel was profiled at USC taps into this specific anxiety, raising concerns about whether the campus environment is truly inclusive or whether the same prejudices that plague the streets have seeped into the ivory tower.

Taraji P. Henson’s decision to speak out is a testament to the power of celebrity influence when wielded responsibly. While some might argue that her status brings a level of attention that ordinary citizens do not receive, it is precisely this attention that forces institutions to act. Without her voice, the incident might have been dismissed as a routine stop, filed away and forgotten. Instead, it has become a catalyst for internal review and public debate. It forces USC to confront the reality of how its security forces interact with the student body and forces the public to reckon with the fact that racial profiling is not confined to any specific socioeconomic class. Even the son of a Hollywood star is not immune.

Furthermore, the involvement of Chief Thomas adds a layer of complexity to the narrative. As a Black man in a position of power within law enforcement, his admission of experiencing profiling underscores the pervasiveness of the issue. It challenges the "us versus them" mentality that often characterizes discussions about police brutality and bias. It suggests that the problem is not simply about "bad apples" within a police force, but about a systemic issue that requires systemic solutions. His pledge to investigate is not just a procedural necessity; it is a moral imperative. He understands that if profiling can happen to the son of a celebrity, it can happen to anyone, and without accountability, trust between the police and the community they serve will remain fractured.

As the investigation proceeds, there will be a spotlight on the methods used by USC Public Safety. The community will be looking for transparency. They will want to know if the officers had probable cause, if their conduct was professional, and if there is a pattern of behavior that suggests a need for retraining or policy reform. The university has an opportunity here to set an example for other institutions facing similar challenges. By handling this investigation with rigor and honesty, USC can demonstrate that it values the safety and dignity of its Black students as much as its reputation.

In the meantime, Taraji P. Henson’s revelation serves as a sobering reminder of the work that remains to be done. It is a call to action for universities, police departments, and society at large to examine the biases that shape our interactions. It is a plea for empathy and understanding. It is a mother’s defense of her son, transformed into a defense of all sons and daughters who face the indignity of being judged by the color of their skin rather than the content of their character. The outcome of Chief Thomas’s investigation remains to be seen, but the conversation that Taraji P. Henson has started is one that must continue until the promise of equal treatment under the law is a reality for everyone, everywhere. The eyes of the nation are watching, waiting to see if justice will not only be done, but will be seen to be done, in the halls of the University of Southern California.
